title: St. Peter II of Alexandria, St. Basilissa, St. Anastasia of Rome & St. Martianus the Ascetic
narrative:
St. Peter II Patriarch of Alexandria, St. Basilissa the Martyr, St. Anastasia of Rome, and St. Martianus the Ascetic

On the nineteenth day of Yekatit, the holy Church meditates upon four lives in which the grace of perseverance takes four different forms: the perseverance of the episcopate under theological assault, the perseverance of the martyr under physical torment, the perseverance of the noblewoman who gave away everything, and the perseverance of the solitary who maintained his post through decades of unseen warfare. Saint Peter the Second of Alexandria, the thirty-second Pope of the apostolic see of Saint Mark, governed the Alexandrian church in the turbulent decades of the post-Chalcedonian crisis, when imperial pressure, popular unrest, and theological controversy combined to make the patriarchal office a place of constant trial. Peter maintained the Miaphysite confession with steadiness, refusing both the intimidation of the imperial court and the temptation to purchase peace by theological compromise, and his tenure strengthened the Alexandrian tradition in a period when it might easily have been destabilised.

Saint Basilissa the Martyr is one of the most beloved of the early Roman martyrs commemorated in the Eastern tradition: according to the Synaxarium, she was a young noblewoman betrothed to a Christian named Julian who, on the night of their wedding, persuaded her husband that they should both consecrate their virginity to God. The couple lived thereafter as brother and sister, devoted to prayer and the care of the poor, until the persecution claimed Julian and eventually Basilissa herself. What the tradition most treasures in her story is the quality of her final testimony: that she died with such peace and joy that the soldiers assigned to execute her wept, and several were converted on the spot. The same pattern noted in Fausta of Cyzicus recurs: the martyr's death is not the end of the mission but its most concentrated moment of communication.

Saint Anastasia of Rome is this day's figure of social and spiritual discovery — a wealthy Roman noblewoman who, after the death of her husband who had persecuted her for her faith, devoted herself entirely to the service of the imprisoned Christians of Rome during the Diocletianic persecution, selling her possessions to support them, visiting them in their cells, tending their wounds, and burying the bodies of those who died. She was eventually arrested, imprisoned, and after prolonged suffering drowned at sea when she was placed on a boat that was to be sunk with its Christian cargo. Her wealth had become a weapon of mercy, and her death was the final and complete divestiture of everything she had given away piece by piece.

Saint Martianus the Ascetic of Caesarea in Palestine brings this assembly to its close with a life of extreme solitary consecration — a hermit who spent decades in a cave near Caesarea, emerging only to receive the Eucharist and returning immediately to his prayer. The tradition records that Martianus was sorely tried by temptation throughout his life, that the warfare never became easier, and that he never pretended otherwise to those who sought his counsel. His greatness lay not in having overcome the passions but in having persisted in the battle through every defeat, returning each time to the same posture of prayer, trusting that God who sees the effort honours it more than the achievement. Peter who held his throne, Basilissa who died in joy, Anastasia who gave everything away, Martianus who never stopped fighting: four images of the one perseverance. Amen.
